title: The SIMPLE Strategy That Made Him Turn $1,600 Into $350 MILLION

segments.text: This is Richard Dennis.
He is one of the most famous traders
who made millions of dollars in the 70s and 80s.
To be specific, at the age of 22,
he borrowed $1,600 that he would turn into $350 million.
Are you serious right now?
Yes, you heard me right, $350 million.
And what's even crazier?
He did all of this in the span of nine years.
That's insane.
He applied a simple and unorthodox strategy
that so many other traders criticized Dennis for.
Moreover, William Eckhart,
another great successful trader of that era,
claimed that trading cannot be taught.
It's only an innate talent.
Dennis believed that anyone could learn to trade
if adequately trained.
To prove his thesis, he launched an experiment
that would become legendary in trading history.
He recruited and trained 21 men and two women.
They came from completely different backgrounds,
clerks, musicians, chess players,
ordinary people with no trading experience.
This group became known as the Turtle Traders.
When the experiment ended five years later,
his Turtles had generated a total profit of $175 million.
Dennis's students achieved incredible results,
proving that discipline beats talent every time.
In fact, Dennis expected to lose on most of his trades,
but when trades went right,
they would run for weeks or even months,
and that's what made the strategy wildly profitable.
Today, several of these former Turtle Traders
have become successful hedge fund managers like Jerry Parker,
and many had already reached millionaire status.
Now, if you're like me, you're thinking,
how did he do it?
Well, I decided to look for the answer
to that specific question,
scavenging the internet and books
for the strategy he used to get those massive gains,
and oh, did I find something really interesting,
and I gotta say, Richard is an absolute genius.
I'm gonna share it with you guys,
so leave me a thank you in the comments.
Let's get straight to it.
Before jumping directly into Richard Dennis's strategies,
we need to discuss the core principles
he used in the trading world.
Risk a fixed percentage per trade.
Cut your losses quickly.
Let your profits run.
Trade trends, not predictions.
Richard always told his students
they should risk no more than 2% of their account
on each trade.
This type of percentage has always been variable
over the years, since it depends heavily
on the strategy, timeframe, asset, and many other variables.
But Dennis used this type of risk management
as his foundation.
The main part of his theories
was based on one crucial concept.
Cut losses as quickly as possible
and let profits run.
This is what separates the professionals
from everyone else.
Let me show you exactly how this works on a chart.
Here we can detect a major resistance zone
where price has touched multiple times
and projected the asset downward
each time it reached that zone.
It fell every single time until obviously,
there comes a moment where the market manages
to break this structure.
What happens then?
The trend explodes upward.
This is something Dennis wanted his students
to understand crystal clear,
that concept that wherever you enter the market,
you can make money as long as you have
proper risk management.
Or as he said, risk management based more
on numbers and mathematics
than on other trader psychology.
Let me give you a very simple example
using this same structure.
Imagine a beginner trader.
Instead of placing this major resistance zone
at this order block, this liquidity zone,
whatever you wanna call it,
sees two highs and decides to place
that resistance line here.
Let's put it in red so everything matches better.
He waits for it to break, for example here,
to enter one of his trades.
Of course, he'd lose this one here,
lose the next one again, and so on
until there comes a moment right at this candle
where this breakout candle marks the beginning
of a major uptrend.
If we go back to the basic concepts from Dennis
that we discussed a moment ago,
if we really focus on having good risk management
and cutting our losses early,
but letting our profits run throughout the entire trend,
here we could have lost, for example, this amount,
then another amount, those are our total losses.
But here, if you notice the proportion,
we would have gained this significant amount.
What's the net result?
Total gains minus total losses equals this profit.
This is a very simple example
so you understand the philosophy Dennis had.
Obviously, he didn't enter randomly all the time.
He followed specific strategies
and considered certain variables that we'll see next.
But clearly, the most important thing
about his trading style and philosophy
is that we can lose small amounts many times
and compensate by winning big amounts few times.
This is one of those things
that if you're starting in the trading world,
might shock you quite a bit.
You don't need a positive win rate to be profitable.
You can lose, for example, nine trades with a 1% stop loss
and then on that one remaining trade out of 10,
make 25% and you'll have made a lot of money.
Now, imagine performing this style of trading consistently,
not just having a track record of 10 trades,
but having one of 1,000, 10,000 or a million trades.
If you look at any chart,
the market will always move the same way.
Trend, consolidation zone or range as we call it,
reversal or in this case, trend continuation,
then another consolidation zone,
another breakout and so on repeatedly.
So it's very logical to think that if we have risk management
that involves cutting those losses as quickly as possible,
when we catch a complete trend in just one of these trades,
we'll make much more money than we lost in all the others.
But what's the problem with all this?
Normally traders or directly people, human beings,
have pretty poor psychological capacity
to face this type of situation.
Any person who doesn't have discipline, practice
and knowledge of the subject will get nervous
or suffer tremendously each time
they have one of these losses.
Even if you tell them they're controlled losses
and that they'll recover and make more money
when they catch the entire trend.
Dennis commented on something
that many other billionaire traders today have also said,
that psychological part of human beings
is what normally makes new traders lose the most money.
Even having professionals helping them,
telling them to follow those methods,
if they don't spend a lot of time gaining experience,
they'll end up losing money for this reason.
Now let's see step by step
how Richard Dennis specifically used price action
to go from $1,600 to $350 million.
As I mentioned before in the structure example,
Dennis, by basing himself on price action

had to pay close attention to previous structural highs

Until it breaks again down here and so on, successively.

a very simple and well-known indicator.

We'll click it and as you can see,

We also set it to 20 and here in source,
we leave it on ATR so it's giving us the data from that ATR.
In the style section,
you can change the colors to your liking.
I'm going to leave it like this in white

So this would be the ATR and here the moving average.
There it is, 2020.
Dennis's basic strategy was based on this.
We detect these previous highs and having this indicator,
